content.text: Based on the official documentation and industry best practices for long-running agent workflows,
"Checkpointing and resuming long agent runs (Managed Agent Runtime pattern)"
persisting agent state at regular intervals to durable storage, enabling the agent to resume from the last checkpoint after crashes rather than restarting from scratch
— reducing hours of lost work to a几分钟 resume.

attrs.alt: Checkpointing and resuming long agent runs (Managed Agent Runtime pattern) concept diagram

content.text: "Per-step durable write of agent state (scratchpad, plan, prior tool returns), keyed by session, resumable from any step"

Agent loop that runs for hours or days
    ↓
Crashes, hits rate limit, or gets rebooted
    ↓
State is in-process (memory)
    ↓
Start over from scratch
: "Every long-running agent loses time and money on retries".

Agent step N → state(N) → Checkpoint to durable storage (workspace/DB)
    ↓
Crash happens
    ↓
New process → load(session, last_step) → resume from N+1
: "With checkpointing, crashes become a rounding error".
Cheap enough to checkpoint every step
Replay or fork from any step
: "Checkpoint per step; resume by session id".
"Treat your agent like a long-running server process, not a request handler"

Scenario: Process 200 documents over 3 days
    ↓
Checkpoint after every batch (e.g., 10 documents)
    ↓
Document 201 fails at 2:30am
    ↓
Without checkpointing: Restart from document 1 (lose 2 days of work)
    ↓
With checkpointing: Resume from document 201 (lose 10 documents)
: "Most common failure mode in multi-day workflows is context loss".

# Agent step N
state = {
    "scratchpad": current_scratchpad,
    "plan": current_plan,
    "tool_returns": prior_tool_results,
    "step_number": N,
    "timestamp": datetime.now()
}

# Persist to durable storage
checkpoint_store.save(
    session_id=session_id,
    step_number=N,
    state=state
)

# Load last checkpoint
checkpoint = checkpoint_store.load(
    session_id=session_id,
    step_number=checkpoint_store.get_last_step(session_id)
)

# Resume agent
agent.state = checkpoint.state
agent.step_number = checkpoint.step_number + 1

# Continue processing
agent.run()
: "Resume is a load, not a rebuild".

1. Set TTL-based expiration:
   Workflows that never complete eventually age out
    ↓
2. Explicit cleanup after completion:
   Reclaim space immediately after successful completion
    ↓
3. Monitor checkpoint store health:
   Storage growth or access latency surfaces before recovery fails
: "Lifecycle management keeps the checkpoint store from growing without bound".
Option 1: Managed Session Storage (Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Runtime)

AgentCore Runtime supports long-running workloads
    ↓
Managed session storage persists filesystem state
    ↓
State persists across stop and resume cycles

Step Functions persists execution state at every transition
    ↓
Enables restart from point of failure (not beginning)
    ↓
Redrive capability from specific failure points
: Dynamic workflows driven by supervisor agents, workflow-stage-aware checkpointing.
Option 3: Custom Checkpointing (Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Memory)

Use AgentCore Memory for custom checkpoint state
    ↓
Persist checkpoint state and specification context
    ↓
For agents with bespoke checkpointing needs
"Checkpointing is only useful if recovery is safe, and recovery is only safe if steps are idempotent"

Idempotent step: produces same result whether it runs once or five times with same input
    ↓
Retry or resume doesn't add duplicate side effects
: "Checkpoint-based recovery can produce duplicate side effects or data corruption".
: "Design each step to be idempotent before implementing checkpointing".
Resume starts from last checkpoint, not from beginning
Force complete restarts on any failure
Duplicate side effects or data corruption on resume
save points in a video game

: if you're playing a 3-hour game and your computer crashes, you don't restart from the beginning (document 1). Instead, you load the last save point (checkpoint after document 200) and continue from there. The checkpoint is durable storage (your save file), the session ID is the game instance, and resuming is loading the save—not rebuilding the game from scratch.
Checkpointing = per-step durable write of agent state (scratchpad, plan, tool returns), keyed by session
Without checkpointing: crashes mean restart from scratch, lose time and money
With checkpointing: resume from last checkpoint, crashes become rounding error
Key properties: per-step writes, durable storage, session keys, versioned state, cross-runtime
Treat agent like long-running server process, not request handler
Checkpoint after every batch, not after every document
Resume from document 201 (where failed), not document 1 (restart)
Resume is a load, not a rebuild
Checkpoint lifecycle: TTL expiration + explicit cleanup after completion
Critical: steps must be idempotent (same result running once or five times)
Idempotency: use keys, conditional writes, event deduplication
Implementation: AgentCore Runtime (filesystem), Step Functions (workflow-state), AgentCore Memory (custom)
Benefits: restart cost drops, duplicate work prevented, compute efficiency improves
Anti-patterns: no persistence, checkpoints without idempotency, skip cleanup

"Long-running workflows without checkpoints pay the full restart cost for every failure, no matter how late it happens. Persisting state at natural boundaries and designing every step to be idempotent lets an agent resume from the last completed checkpoint rather than redo work".
When working with checkpointing and resuming long agent runs (managed agent runtime pattern), following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with checkpointing and resuming long agent runs (managed agent runtime pattern). Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of checkpointing and resuming long agent runs (managed agent runtime pattern) scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
